description: Behavior cases for async job/worker execution design: per-job idempotency + retry + visibility timeout + DLQ, resumable long jobs, per-tenant fairness, scheduling with overlap prevention, and the transport-vs-execution and sync-write boundaries.

cases.prompt: Generating a large export blocks the request for 40 seconds and sometimes times out. Move it to a background job and design how the worker runs it, retries, and reports back.

assertions: Catalogs the job with its trigger, duration, side effects, and whether it is safe to re-run, then designs an execution contract: idempotency, retry with backoff and a retryable-vs-poison classifier, a visibility timeout sized above real runtime, and a DLQ with a named owner.
Designs resumability/checkpointing so a crash mid-export resumes rather than restarting from zero or double-charging.
Provides a way to report success/failure back to the user rather than fire-and-forget.
Defers the message transport (topics/partitions/delivery semantics) to streaming-event-architect and capacity validation to the performance/load skills.

cases.prompt: When one big customer kicks off a bulk operation with tens of thousands of jobs, every other customer's jobs stall behind them. Fix the worker design so one tenant can't starve the others.

assertions: Diagnoses the single undifferentiated queue as the cause and designs per-tenant fairness: per-tenant queues with weighted/round-robin draining, per-tenant concurrency caps, or priority lanes for interactive vs bulk work.
States explicitly how a tenant's flood is bounded so others keep flowing — noisy-neighbor defense at the worker layer.
Adds per-tenant queue-depth as a first-class observability signal with a threshold and owner.
Does not merely add more workers, which scales the starvation rather than removing the unfairness.

cases.prompt: Some jobs retry forever and hammer a third-party API, and separately a long job sometimes runs twice at the same time. What's wrong with our job handling?

assertions: Identifies retry-forever as a missing poison classifier: permanent failures (bad input, 4xx) must be dead-lettered fast, not retried indefinitely with backoff.
Identifies the double-run as a visibility timeout shorter than the job's real runtime — the queue redelivers while the first worker is still processing — and sizes the lease above runtime.
Requires idempotency so any redelivery that does happen cannot double-apply.
Adds DLQ ownership and a fill alert so dead-lettered jobs are not lost silently.

cases.prompt: The role-change endpoint feels slow. Just push the whole authorize-and-update-permissions operation onto a background queue so the response returns instantly.

assertions: Pushes back: a synchronous, user-blocking, security-sensitive write does not belong on a background queue just to speed up the response — that can hide authorization outcomes and lose the user's result.
Routes the write to command-gateway-architect's synchronous protected path and suggests optimizing it there, or making only a genuinely deferrable side effect async.
Names the risk: a fire-and-forget enqueue that silently fails is worse than a slow but honest synchronous call for a permission change.

cases.prompt: Design our event backbone: which flows are streams vs queues, the partition keys, consumer-group layout, delivery semantics, and CDC from the operational database.

assertions: Does not invoke background-job-orchestration-architect: stream-vs-queue, partition keys, consumer groups, delivery semantics, and CDC are the message transport owned by streaming-event-architect.
Jobs may ride that transport, but nothing here asks for the worker execution contract.
